params.text:
Ill-fitting dentures and referral

Dentures that no longer fit well are a frequent and easily missed problem. Over time a person's mouth changes shape, especially after weight loss, and a denture that once fitted can become loose, rub and cause sores. Ill-fitting dentures can stop a person eating properly and knock their confidence.

  • Signs of poor fit: dentures that slip, click, rub or leave sore, red patches.
  • Effects: difficulty eating and speaking, mouth ulcers and reluctance to wear them.
  • Action: report the problem and arrange a dental review — do not try to adjust the denture yourself.

A dentist can reline, adjust or replace a denture, restoring comfort and the ability to eat well.

When to refer to a dentist

You give daily mouth care, but the assessment and treatment of the mouth belong to dental professionals. NICE guidance encourages supporting people to keep access to a dentist and regular dental reviews. Knowing when to seek that help is part of your role. Report and arrange a dental opinion when you notice warning signs, and treat pain, bleeding or a person unable to eat as reasons to act promptly.

Reasons to refer include: toothache or mouth pain; bleeding or swollen gums that do not settle; a mouth ulcer or sore patch lasting around three weeks or more; loose, broken or lost teeth; ill-fitting, cracked or lost dentures; signs of infection such as thrush or swelling; and any difficulty eating because of the mouth. When you are unsure, it is always safer to report and let a professional decide. You are the eyes and ears; the dentist makes the diagnosis and provides the treatment. Record what you noticed and what was reported, so nothing is lost.

params.question: A person has lost weight and their dentures now slip and rub, leaving a sore patch. What should you do?

answers.text: Report the problem and arrange a dental review, keeping the mouth clean and comfortable meanwhile

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Ill-fitting dentures need a dentist to adjust, reline or replace them; you report and support comfort.

answers.text: File down or adjust the denture yourself so it fits better

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Adjusting dentures is a dental task; doing it yourself could damage the denture and harm the person.

answers.text: Tell the person to keep wearing them and get used to the soreness

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. Sore, ill-fitting dentures should be reviewed by a dentist, not endured.

answers.text: Throw the dentures away so the person no longer has the problem

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Removing a person's dentures leaves them unable to eat and speak properly; the answer is a dental review.
